evidence_quotes.quote: lack of consumer protection for video games as digital goods
shutting down servers and rendering purchased games unplayable
Video games are digital goods under EU law
exploitative EULAs allowing unilateral revocation of access

paragraphs.text: As a volunteer in the Stop Killing Games European Citizens Initiative (ECI), I submit this feedback urging the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) to address the lack of consumer protection for video games as digital goods. This ECI, led by YouTuber Ross Scott (Accursed Farms, https://www.youtube.com/@AccursedFarms ), has surpassed 1.4 million signatures across the EU, with strong support from Portugal and other member states. It calls on lawmakers to ensure that publishers keep games playable even after official support ends, embedding this principle in EU law to safeguard digital consumers. Our campaign opposes the practice of publishers shutting down servers and rendering purchased games unplayable, turning them into digital bombs. This violates the basic sales contract: when consumers pay for a game, they expect it to endure. We ask only that core gameplay remains functional, via private servers, offline modes, or open-source code, or, failing that, that publishers clearly label games with an expiration warning. This is not overregulation, but a demand for transparency and durability, much like expecting a book to remain readable after printing stops. The initiative extends globally, with petitions in the UK, Australia, and Brazil. Cases like Ubisofts shutdown of The Crew show how remote deactivation harms millions worldwide. In Portugal, weve reached only 51% of our national target, but the DFA can help elevate awareness and set stronger EU-wide standards. Industry lobbyists such as Video Games Europe misrepresent our goals as too costly, ignoring that the sector, worth billions, already uses exploitative EULAs allowing unilateral revocation of access. The DFA must close this loophole so that EULAs reflect fair, enforceable contracts. Video games are digital goods under EU law and deserve the same durability standards as physical products. With the EUs Brussels Effect, these protections could set a global precedent, ensuring lasting consumer rights in a digital-first economy. Claims of excessive costs are unfounded. Like seatbelts in cars or expiry labeling in food, consumer protection measures strengthen industries in the long run. The proposal is non-retroactive and allows a 23 year adaptation period; future games designed responsibly would incur no added cost. With over 1.4 million signatures, the Commission is now obliged to consider our proposal. We urge the DFA to enshrine these protections and make Europe a leader in digital fairness. I remain at your disposal for further clarification or discussion. I must also express my strong disdain for the growing wave of legislative and regulatory measures that, under the guise of protecting children, aim to extend surveillance and control over the privacy of entire populations. This recurring narrative, the emotionally charged think of the children appeal, is increasingly being weaponized as a political and moral shield to justify invasive technologies and intrusive policies that undermine digital rights. Behind the veneer of concern for minors lies a much broader agenda: the normalization of identity verification systems, age-gating frameworks, and even direct access to private communications, all under the pretext of safety. Such measures pave the way for state-sanctioned mass surveillance and corporate data exploitation, with little to no evidence that they actually protect children from harm. In practice, they erode the fundamental right to privacy for everyone, including the very youth they claim to defend. It is essential to recognize that privacy is not the enemy of child protection; it is one of its pillars. A society that treats all citizens as potential suspects does not become safer; it becomes more fearful and less free. True protection of minors comes through education, digital literacy, responsible parenting, and the enforcement of existing laws against exploitation and abuse, not through dismantling the infrastructure of private communication for all.
